data.title: Categorizing Tech Companies' Valuation Risk
data.description:
With market valuations at such a high level today, perhaps the biggest question on the minds of investors is: Are valuations too high? The answer, of course, varies by company.

I am primarily a tech company investor, and to gain insight into this, I examined the largest tech companies by valuation and calculated their P/E Growth Ratios. I was frankly surprised by the result, which gave me pause to reconsider some of my investments.

I previously used the PEG ratio to justify NVIDIA's valuation (Nvidia: It's All About Growth), and decided to expand this analysis to compare it to others.

PEG Ratio Explanation

Some readers may not be familiar with the use of P/E growth ratios, so a brief explanation is provided below.

PEG Ratio Formula

PEG = PE / Expected EPS Growth

So if a company has a P/E of 25 and is expected to grow earnings at 15% annually, PEG=25/15=1.67

PEG Origins

The PEG ratio was created by legendary investor Peter Lynch (The PEG Ratio, Peter Lynch’s Favorite Investing Metric) for this very purpose of evaluating companies with different P/E and growth expectations:

Peter Lynch, the former Fidelity Fund manager with one of the best track records in mutual fund history, popularized the GARP style of investing while running the Magellan fund. Lynch would often look for growth companies benefiting from a market trend. Frequently, these stocks appeared expensive when viewed through a standard valuation multiple, such as the price-to-earnings ratio, but ultimately proved to be relatively cheap in terms of their growth.

To better analyze growth companies, Lynch used the PEG ratio, which is calculated by dividing the price-to-earnings ratio by the firm’s growth rate. This was one of Lynch’s favorite stock investing criteria; here's how it works:

Assume we are comparing two different stocks, both of which have a price-to-earnings ratio of 30. On the surface, they may look the same, but now let’s look at the growth rates. Let's say Company A is growing earnings at 40% while Company B is growing earnings at 15%. Company A has a PEG (price-to-earnings to growth rate) of 0.75 (30 divided by 40 equals 0.75) while Company B has a PEG of 2.0 (30 divided by 15 equals 2).

PEG Benchmark

The lower the PEG, the more attractive the company's valuation is relative to its growth rate. For Lynch, a PEG of 1.0 or less was considered very good. P/E ratios were somewhat lower at that time, so it's possible that something a little higher than 1.0 may be an appropriate benchmark.

Example: Years to Normal PE Ratio

To illustrate how this works, we can model the time it will take for the current P/E ratio to reach what is expected to be normal based on the growth rate implied by the PEG. Here is an example for a $35 stock currently trading at a 35 P/E (the median of the evaluation group below). I model four different PEG ratios, ranging from 3.0 to 1.0.

For the sake of this model, I've used 25 P/E as "normal" P/E. You could use a different target normal ratio.

The chart shows the differences in potential return at various PEGs, assuming the P/E ratio eventually reaches its normal level. A 3.0 PEG won't hit the normal P/E ratio until Year 4 and will only produce a CAGR of 1.36%, while a PEG of 1.0 reaches the normal in Year 1 and yields a CAGR of 12.35%. A PEG of 1.5 also provides a reasonable CAGR.

PEG Comparisons

When I computed the PEG for the 18 largest tech companies, I was surprised by the differences. So, I thought it would be helpful to share them.

There was a wide disparity of PEG ratios, with a median of 2.0. The median P/E ratio was 35.8, but the average was significantly higher at 52.6, primarily due to a few extremely high P/E ratios. The average and median growth rates were close at 16%-17%.

I classified them into five categories:

  1. Dark Green - These have a PEG of less than 1.5, so they have a P/E ratio reasonably balanced to their expected growth. These appear to be the most promising investment opportunities. The two with the highest P/E ratios AMD and NVDA, both have higher expected growth rates. I own all four of these stocks (including TSM and GOOGL) and will continue to hold them, possibly increasing my position.
  2. Light Green - These five companies have a higher PEG ratio but are still below the median of 2.0. Most have a reasonable P/E ratio, except AVGO, which justifies it with a higher growth rate. I own four of the five and will continue to own them, but I’ll keep an eye on them.
  3. Grey - These four stocks are in a grey area with a PEG ratio above 2.0 (the median) but below 3.0. I own all four of these stocks and will consider reducing my exposure to them in case of a market decline.
  4. Orange - With a PEG of more than 3.0, these companies are in a danger zone. I own one of them (MSFT) and will look at it more closely.
  5. Red – With a PEG greater than 8.0, I consider these stocks to have outrageous P/E ratios not justified by their growth expectations. This roughly means that they will need to grow 8X their current expected growth rates to “grow into” their current P/E ratio. I don't own either of them, but I have a short position in TSLA.

In addition to evaluating these valuations against the benchmark, it is also helpful to compare them to each other. In a comparison, you see that the first two categories may be less risky and have more upside potential than the others, particularly the high-risk companies in the red category.

Methodology

If you are interested in the calculation of the data in the table, here is an explanation:

  • All data are sourced from Seeking Alpha, including P/E ratios, market capitalizations, and growth rates.
  • The P/E ratios are forward.
  • The growth ratios represent the forward 3-5-year GAAP EPS growth rates, as projected by Seeking Alpha.
  • I choose to use the 3–5-year growth rate instead of the growth rate for the coming year. This provides a more comprehensive, long-term valuation assessment. If you use the upcoming year’s growth rate, some companies will fall below a 1.0 PEG. For example, NVDA would be 0.61 (39.7/65.0).
  • You can compute the PEG differently, such as using the current P/E without forward-looking estimates and historical growth rates. The ratios would be different, but the comparisons would be somewhat similar.
  • You can also include dividend payments in the P/E ratio, not the PEG index. Only two companies on the list have significant dividend yields: IBM (2.4%) and CSCO (2.4%). IBM's adjusted PEG would be 25.9/ (6.9 + 2.4) or 2.78. Cisco would be 17.8 / (6.3 + 2.4), or 2.04. This adjustment would make each of these a little more attractive as an investment.

Applying The PEG Ratio

You shouldn’t just take these results blindly. Instead, they should serve as a starting point in assessing valuation risk in your portfolio. The most critical factor in the equation is the assumptions for EPS growth rates. Those from Seeking Alpha are a good starting point, but you should use your own assumptions and judgment to inform your analysis. For example, if you assume a 150% annual EPS growth rate for Tesla over the next 3-5 years, you would get a more reasonable 1.2 PEG ratio.

data.title: PNC Financial Vs. U.S. Bancorp: A Dividend Investor's Dilemma
data.description:
PNC Financial Services (NYSE:PNC) and U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) are top regional banks for their national reach and stability. Both are good options for dividend investors in a diversified portfolio. But if you're choosing between them, there are some key differences to consider: geographic exposure, revenue mix, and their digital offerings.

My midyear review breaks down how they’re situated looking toward year-end and offers a valuation-based verdict. I rate USB a Buy at a forward P/B of ~1.2, while PNC's recent outperformance moves it to a Hold.

Where the Business Models Diverge

Seeking Alpha’s Virtual Analyst Reports for PNC and for USB give overviews of their business models and Quant ratings. I’ll start with a quick refresher, but those already familiar may wish to skip ahead.

PNC and USB are the two biggest banks outside the G-SIB or “megabank” tier. Both do nearly all their business domestically and fund themselves mainly through deposits. PNC's revenue comes more from interest-based products: business loans, commercial real estate lending, residential mortgages, etc. It also offers treasury and investment services to larger companies. USB earns more on fee income from payment processing, credit card services, and corporate trusts. It has fewer physical branches but has a more developed digital banking platform.

Physical footprints: PNC operates mainly in the Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, and Southeast regions and USB in the upper Midwest and West, especially California.

First-Half Performance (2025)

Q1 and Q2 earnings showed steady fundamentals, even as margins dipped across the board. Credit performance has remained stable, and neither bank has drastically revised its full-year outlook.

While the stocks track closely, PNC has outperformed USB by ~8.5% over the past year:

H1 2025 highlights:

PNC generated just over $2B in net income for the first half of the year, translating to $6.98 per share (an increase from $6.21 at this time last year). Its NIM edged lower from 2.78% in Q1 to 2.75% in Q2, and ROTCE eased from 14% to 13.7%. Operating costs didn’t move much in Q1 but fell by about 3% YoY in Q2. On the capital side, PNC’s CET1 ratio was up to 10.7% compared to 9.5% a year earlier. Management expects slight declines of 1–2% for revenue, expenses, and NII through the rest of the year.

USB brought in net income of $3.3B, translating to $2.01/share (up from $1.63 in H1 2024). Like PNC, it saw a small drop in NIM (from 2.72% to 2.70%) and a pullback in ROTCE which landed at 16.4%, down from 17.5%. Expenses declined both from Q1 and compared to last year. USB’s CET1 rose to 10.8%, well above the 8.5% from a year ago. Its full-year forecast hasn't changed much, though the outlook for NII is now a bit softer: a 3–4% drop is expected instead of the previous 2–3% range.

Both banks have been dealing with deposit shifts, as more clients move into interest-bearing accounts. USB appears to be handling this better with slower growth in interest expense.

Note that both have underperformed the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) this year on a total return basis, USB especially.

What to Watch for in the Second Half and Beyond

1. Digital Initiatives

USB just expanded its partnership with Fiserv to let Elan clients start using new credit cards immediately through their phones. This should boost sign-ups and reach customers where they’re already doing business – fintech apps and checkout screens. USB’s mobile and online platforms are well rated (e.g., earning a top spot in J.D. Power’s 2025 Online Banking Satisfaction Study).

PNC has been working on out its own upgrades, recently partnering with Atomic to allow customers to switch direct deposits within the app – a move to convert users into primary account holders. It’s also expanding use of APIs (application programming interfaces), e.g., allowing business clients to connect to PNC’s treasury platform. Management has also mentioned upgrades to its mortgage automation tools, but they’ve not disclosed adoption numbers.

** USB has the lead here as its mobile platforms are further along.

2. Geographic Reach

PNC is expanding in regions with growing populations, e.g., Arizona, Texas, and Florida. This should support future demand for consumer and business loans. USB’s expansion has been mainly on the West Coast. Its 2022 acquisition of MUFG's Union Bank added $80B+ in deposits, mostly in California and the Pacific Northwest. These areas have higher incomes and business density but have seen slower deposit growth.

** I like PNC’s concentration in regions with strong population and employment trends.

3. Partnerships

USB’s partnership with State Farm is pretty unique. Launched in 2020, it has added 900,000+ new accounts by marketing USB products through State Farm’s network of insurance agents, giving the bank additional reach beyond its branches. Management has highlighted this partnership as a key driver of account growth.

PNC has tended to favor business partnerships over retail, developing tools for companies to help manage cash flow, access credit, and expand internationally. Management has pointed to growth in syndicated loans and cross-border payments as evidence of success.

** I see retail (NYSE:USB) vs. commercial reach (NYSE:PNC) as equal strengths.

4. Credit and Risk Management

Both reported healthy credit metrics in this week’s Q2 reports. USB added to its reserves for office-related commercial real estate while PNC reported a small uptick in charge-offs. But nonperforming assets have held steady and neither management team flagged major concerns in the earnings calls.

USB is more exposed to consumer credit while PNC’s loan book is weighted more toward commercial and corporate lending:

  • USB – 40–45% consumer, 55–60% commercial/corporate
  • PNC – 25–30% consumer, 70–75% commercial/corporate

PNC’s loan book may be more defensive if consumer finances deteriorate, but I like that USB earns a larger share of revenue through fees as that may help offset tighter margins.

** For me, USB’s revenue mix gives it a slight edge.

Shareholder Returns: Dividends and Buybacks

Both offer attractive returns pairing dividend growth with share repurchases. PNC just raised its dividend by 6%, and its payout ratio leaves plenty of room to extend its growth streak. PNC also repurchased $200M in stock, worth a buyback yield of 0.84% (per Morningstar). Altogether, PNC's shareholder yield sits at around 4.35%.

USB offers the larger dividend but the 5Y CAGR is about half that of PNC’s. It has the same 14-year streak of increases and a comparable payout ratio. USB authorized a $5B buyback in 2024 but has hardly tapped it thus far. With the CET1 ratio now above target, management suggests buybacks will ramp up in late 2025 or early 2026.

Advantage: PNC – as a long-minded investor, I prefer faster dividend growth.

Valuation and Price Targets

PNC is trading near its historical averages for P/E and P/B while USB looks undervalued relative to its norms and sector medians. The trailing dividend yields also suggest a favorable valuation for USB.

Analyst sentiment is positive for both. USB has a consensus Buy rating and a price target of $52.83 for about 18% implied upside. PNC also has a Buy rating, with a $211.88 target implying ~9% upside.

Advantage: USB – its multiples are a bit more attractive despite similar growth.

Risks to Monitor

  • Capital rules – Looser requirements might free up cash for buybacks, dividend increases, and reinvestment, but if easing doesn’t materialize as expected, these could be constrained.
  • Commercial real estate – Office markets in places like San Francisco, L.A., and Chicago remain weak. If values fall further, both banks may see additional loan losses.
  • Margin pressure – Higher deposit costs are already squeezing NIM. If rates stay elevated, as many expect, margins may drift lower.
  • Muted loan demand – Loan balances were flat in Q2. If demand softens further (especially on the commercial side), it will drag on revenue.

Investor Takeaways

Both banks are fundamentally sound and may suit income-focused investors. But USB's cheaper valuation and diversified fee base give it a bit more upside in my view. If credit trends stay stable, it may have more room to rerate.

PNC is also high-quality with strong credit metrics. Like USB, it’s shown consistent dividend growth and has been an industry leader in recent years. But with the stock up ~10% more than USB in recent months, I find it slightly less attractive.

Either could be a good long-term portfolio piece, but for value and upside, I give USB the slight edge and a Buy rating.

Author’s Note: All figures reflect data available as of July 17, 2025.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Winds Of Change In The U.S. Market
data.description:
British American Tobacco p.l.c. (NYSE:BTI) is arguably the “second-best” tobacco company in the world. Their brands are well known and have top market shares, but they are not Marlboro. Their journey into next generation products (heat-not-burn, vapes and pouches) has yielded profits, the second company to surpass breakeven, but still far behind arch-rival Philip Morris (PM) ("PMI").

I consider PMI a “compounder” with their category making products (IQOS and Zyn) now practically uncatchable, and their multiple rapidly expanding away from the traditional tobacco penalty valuation.

BTI is more of a value play. I intend to keep it in my portfolio mostly for the dividend, which is high-yielding and also growing (a rare combination). All sustainable dividends need to be supported by cash flow. BTI has been a cash flow machine, but has (in the past couple of years) run into trouble in its most important market, the USA.

BTI in America:

BTI’s global footprint is (for once) second to none. On combustibles, they are the only true global player, since PM cannot sell in the USA, where Altria (MO) has the exclusive rights.

I described their business and geographic footprint more fully in a prior note. For this one, I would like to focus on the U.S. market. The region accounts for about half of worldwide profits for BTI; derived from 43% of revenues, by far their most relevant geography and the world’s richest profit pool.

Fighting for share in this most-coveted profit pool are three swimmers, BTI, PM and MO. On a macro basis, the nicotine market (not only in U.S., but particularly acute in the U.S.) is shifting to next-gen products. Cigarette volumes can decline mid to high-single digits any given year and have been offset by matching price increases. This formula for revenue stability has an expiry date; in the extreme we cannot expect the last smoker standing to pay billions a year. Even if Elon picks up the habit, he might just purchase elsewhere – he has a jet after all.

Recognizing this (belatedly, but still…) BTI took a big write-down on their RJ Reynolds’ brands concurrent with CEO Tadeu Marroco stepping up to the role. In addition, the remaining value of U.S. combustible brands is being amortized yearly to also reflect the diminishing intangible value (and their eventual phasing out).

On the next-gen (or reduced risk) products side, the fight is twofold: on Modern Oral, Zyn is the category leader by a wide margin, with Velo trailing somewhere in the dust. On e-cigarettes (or vapes) the fight is between BTI’s VUSE and dozens of illegal Chinese vapes.

There are no alternatives for heat-not-burn, PM has a PMTA authorization pending and is running very limited trials on IQOS at selected cities. My reading is they are focusing on Zyn for the time being, perhaps wondering how big it can grow, knowing they can pull heavily on the IQOS lever later.

The picture is changing for BTI on this side of the Atlantic:

Several developments have turned headwinds (or question marks) into tailwinds (or answers) and in the process appear to be turning BTI’s performance in the USA from “scary” to “hopeful.”

First, not related to the U.S. but close, a settlement was reached with plaintiffs’ on Canada litigation. The big tobacco companies agreed on a court-approved path forward that limits exposure, creates up-front and recurring payments with the latter a portion of combustible profits (apparently not affecting revenues or profits from next generation nicotine). This bring closure to multi-decade fight and removes uncertainty for all stakeholders.

Second, something that did not happen. After years of the FDA threatening to issue an outright ban on menthol, the idea has been shelved for the time being. Such a ban would have harmed BTI disproportionally, mainly due to their reliance on menthol leader Newport for combustible sales. And combustible sales are still necessary to fund new categories (and the dividend!).

Third is progress on modern oral. Velo (through Velo Plus) is gaining some traction vs. Zyn. The differentiated product, with improved packaging, presentation and moisture, is achieving repeat purchases, a key for its introductory phase. Promotional pricing also helps, margins will not be near Zyn for the time being, that will come later as pricing gaps slowly close (trying to read from PM comments, Velo plus is at least 50% discounted vs Zyn).

It is worthwhile reminding readers that Velo (Velo 2.0) is the absolute leader in (much smaller) Scandinavian market. There, market share are routinely in the 80’s% and, in a way, Velo plus is the one hope BTI has in avoiding this happening in the U.S. market for Zyn.

Fourth, and this was a surprise to me, but increased news and commentary is confirming it: FDA, legislators and health authorities appear to be finally getting serious about blocking illegal vapes out of the U.S. market. This is a game changer for BTI more than for anyone else.

Tariffs also may factor in. Here is a chart of the almost immediate impact they had on vape imports earlier this year.

BTI heavily invested and got great early traction on its VUSE vape product some five years ago. It still leads the “tracked channels” by a wide margin. The problem is that the market subsequently became flooded with ultra cheap vapes (mainly from China) taking up more than half the market. The picture at the header a smoke shop is rather typical and illustrates the disrupted state of the market for e-cigs.

In certain states, Louisiana being the prime example, illegal vapes were banned, and VUSE quickly returned to a leadership position there. This is encouraging, however state by state legislation is too slow and implementation of bans is patchy, so the hint of greater federal focus must be very welcome news for BTI.

If anything, federal resources have a much better chance of success in stopping these products coming in. They can disrupt distributors and improve customs seizures. States have a more limited set of tools and usually focus mostly on disincentives for retailers.

Even partial success would be a huge win for VUSE. Regaining a leadership position in vape would go a long way in offsetting their lagging position in modern oral. Since their HnB product is inferior to IQOS (which will eventually be introduced into the market), vaping is the only category where BTI has any chance to lead in the U.S. market (like it once did, before the Chinese flood).

The market seems to have gotten a feel for the change in wind direction and has started to reassess valuation for BTI.

Improving Valuation:

Improving valuation is definitely good for those of us interested in the long term sustainability of cash flows and dividends. These companies all have substantial debt that needs to get refinanced constantly. Low valuation multiples tend to correlate with higher spreads and eventually, in extreme cases, difficulty in refinancing.

While PM is in another league, the traditional discount (on a P/E forward basis) to MO has disappeared for BTI. Trading for as low as 6x forward P/E a year ago, the multiple has improved to over 11x.

Regulation and other Risks:

Of course regulation keeps getting tougher for all companies, especially on combustibles. BTI flagged changes in Australia as having a big impact this year. The market there is one of the most heavily regulated (and also, no surprise, flooded in contraband). It is a case in point that allowing alternatives in a regulated manner is better than trying to ban nicotine out of existence.

The slow speed at which FDA can review PMTA requests is frustrating for companies and investors. Some news articles point to AI being used to help speed up the analysis of the applications and hopefully improve matters. But it is still difficult to invest behind brands that still have some chance of not getting cleared.

Final thoughts

The U.S. market is absolutely core to BTI. The most profitable nicotine market, is also heavily contested and at the moment poorly regulated and disorganized when it comes to non-combustible nicotine.

That may change, or it many change to some degree. Any movement is positive to companies striving to comply. Aside from that, BTI seems to be gaining traction on modern oral in the U.S., a development few expected (including me). It is still very early and we shall see how market shares behave once promotional activity subsides...but what I am saying is "there is a chance."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Western Alliance Bancorporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Western Alliance Bancorporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Verisk Analytics: Too Expensive Following Its Strategy Of Focus
data.description:
In the fall of 2022, I believed that Verisk Analytics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VRSK) was becoming a pure play on insurance analytics following the sale of its financial service and energy unit.

The strategy to focus worked out, as the company has seen solid growth, but moreover has demonstrated margin gains ever since. As a result, the market has been awarding ever higher valuations to the firm. This has pushed up expectations to levels far too high to see any appeal here, as even a potential interesting M&A move is not going to alter that stance.

All About Data Analytics

Verisk serves the global insurance industry, this in itself being a hugely diversified industry which includes segments like property, life & annuity, auto, extreme events, casualty, fraud detection, and others.

The company provides strategic data analytics and is a technology partner to the global insurance industry. Its clients use its services in order to improve their efficiency, improved underwriting, claims and in general make more informed decisions in their businesses.

These services remain in greater demand as time passes, as Verisk has been a solid performer in this industry, with this complemented by execution and solid capita allocation.

By now the company has grown to generate $2.7 billion in sales with EBITDA margins reported at more than 53% of sales, being a 100% focused business which reports retention rates in excess of 95%. The $2.7 billion insurance business is for over 70% comprised out of underwriting solutions, complemented by claim solutions. Despite global operations, the company generates over 80% of sales in the U.S.

The company was not always this focused. Back in 2022, the company generated $2.2 billion in revenues from these core insurance operations, but the business operated a smaller >$600 million energy segment business and a near $150 million financial service industry as well, both reporting much lower margin profiles. The latter business unit was sold in a >$500 million deal to TransUnion (TRU) around that time.

Pro forma sales were seen around $2.85 billion with earnings seen at $666 million, equal to just over $4 per share, with adjusted earnings seen over a dollar per share higher. Commanding a $30 billion enterprise valuation at the time, the company was valued around 10 times sales, 20 times EBITDA and 35 times adjusted earnings, rather demanding multiples.

The company furthermore announced a $3.1 billion deal with Veritas Capital to sell its energy business, with the business valued at 6 times sales and 15 times EBITDA. The idea was to create a focused insurance business which would become unleveraged, if not for substantial share buybacks being planned, yet overall premium valuations made me hesitant to get involved.

What Happened?

Fast forwarding nearly 3 years in time, shares of Verisk have risen nearly 70%, returns which are largely at par compared to the wider S&P 500 (SP500), as shares now trade a few dollars above the $300 mark.

Following the simplification efforts and moves late in 2022, which mostly took place in 2023, the company reported a 7% increase in reported sales for 2023 to $2.68 billion (with growth reported after adjusting for the divestments closed during the year). Continued growth and margin expansion (driven by focusing on higher-margin activities) made that adjusted earnings rose by 14% to $5.71 per share, with adjustments being made looking quite fair.

The company guided for continued gains in 2024, with sales seen up to $2.84-$2.90 billion, with adjusted earnings seen up to $6.30-$6.60 per share. Forwarding to this year, the company perfectly delivered on this, with revenues reported up more than 7% to $2.88 billion and adjusted earnings up another 16% to $6.64 per share. Net debt was reported at $2.77 billion, for a 1.8 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA reported at $1.58 billion.

In a real steady fashion, the company guided for 2025 sales going up further to $3.03-$3.08 billion, with adjusted earnings seen moving up modestly to a midpoint of $6.95 per share. The company reconfirmed this guidance alongside solid first quarter earnings results.

Trading at $303 per share, the 141 million shares of the business are valued at $42.7 billion, for a $45.3 billion enterprise valuation based on the prevailing net debt load. This only shows that the strategy to focus has only created more value, with the business valued around 15 times sales and earnings multiples having increased to the mid-forties here!

A Bolt-On Deal

By July, the company announced a bolt-on acquisition, with the company having reached an agreement to acquire SuranceBay in a $162 million deal. The business provides producer licensing, onboarding and compliance solutions for the life insurance and annuity industry.

The company has not indicated more details on the purchase business, but this is really a bolt-on deal. The transaction is valued at just less than 0.5% of the enterprise valuation of Verisk and likely will contribute a greater percentage to sales here, with more information to be outlined alongside the second quarter earnings results, due by month end.

A Great Business, With An Every Increasing Price

The reality is that an increasing focus has only resulted in higher valuation multiples applied to this business, pushing up earnings multiples to the mid-forties, for an earnings yield in the low 2%s. This is driven by the predictability and durability of the business as the company steadily keeps growing at 7-8%, truly impressive.

There are some potential developments as well. Of course, AI is key in this, as this could provide a growth acceleration to the business, with more opportunities for firms like Verisk to offer more services to its clients. On the other hand, it very well might be a threat as well, with Verisk´s services becoming less special and more accessible.

In either case, the jury is still out on that. It is very clear that the regular increase in valuation multiple and expectations here makes it very easy for me to be very cautious on Verisk Analytics, Inc. shares at these premium multiples.

data.title: The State Of REITs: July 2025 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

In June, REITs continued to recover with a +2.56% average total return. However, the REIT sector again lagged the gains of the broader market with stronger performances from the NASDAQ (+6.6%), S&P 500 (+5.1%) and Dow Jones Industrial Average (+4.5%). The market cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) underperformed the average REIT again in June (+0.69% vs. +2.56%), but has significantly outperformed year-to-date (+2.01% vs. -5.65%). The spread between the 2025 FFO multiples of large cap REITs (17.6x) and small cap REITs (13.3x) narrowed in June as multiples contracted 0.2 turns for large caps but expanded 0.4 turns for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 32.3% more for each dollar of FFO from large cap REITs relative to small cap REITs. In this monthly publication, I will provide REIT data on numerous metrics to help readers identify which property types and individual securities currently offer the best opportunities to achieve their investment goals.

After underperforming in each of the first 5 months of 2025, micro cap REITs (+7.19%) solidly outperformed their larger peers in June. Small caps (+2.99%), mid caps (+1.80%) and large caps (+0.03%) saw smaller gains. During the first half of 2025, large cap REITs have outperformed small caps by 581 basis points.

13 out of 18 Property Types Averaged Positive Returns in June

72.22% of REIT property types averaged a positive total return in June. There was an 11.07% total return spread between the best and worst performing property types. Office (+7.60%) and Single Family Housing (+5.60%) outperformed all other property types in June. Self-Storage (-3.47%) and Timber REITs (-2.49%) were the worst performing REIT property types.

Hotel (-15.35%), Office (-15.27%) and Shopping Center REITs (-13.66%) struggled over the first half of 2025 with double-digit average declines. Health Care (+8.98%) and Casinos (+7.35%) were the top performers over the first half of the year.

The REIT sector as a whole saw the average P/FFO (2025Y) increase 0.1 turns in June from 13.6x up to 13.7x. 50% of property types averaged multiple expansion, 27.8% averaged multiple contraction and 22.2% saw multiples hold steady in June. Data Centers (27.6x), Land (24x), Multifamily (23x), Manufactured Housing (18.8x) and Single Family Housing (18.4x) currently trade at the highest average multiples among REIT property types. Hotels (6.3x), Office (8.9x) and Malls (9.1x) are the only property types that average single digit FFO multiples.

Performance of Individual Securities

Although Wheeler REIT (WHLR) (+52.26%) led the REIT sector in June, this dead cat bounce did little to offset the share price collapse over the first 5 months of the year. Over the first half of 2025, Wheeler’s total return was the worst in the REIT sector with a tragic -98.72%. This continues a multi-year freefall in which Wheeler has consistently severely underperformed.

Presidio Property Trust (SQFT) (-11.36%) continues to struggle and closed out the first half of the year with a -34.62% year-to-date total return. Presidio had fallen out of compliance with NASDAQ’s rule pertaining to the $1.00 minimum bid price requirement, but managed to regain compliance on June 2nd, 2025 via a 1-for-10 reverse stock split.

63.87% of REITs had a positive total return in June. REITs have averaged a -5.65% year-to-date total return in 2025, which is slightly worse than the -3.86% return for the REIT sector over the first half of 2024.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Dividend Yield

Dividend yield is an important component of a REIT's total return. The particularly high dividend yields of the REIT sector are, for many investors, the primary reason for investment in this sector. As many REITs are currently trading at share prices well below their NAV, yields are currently quite high for many REITs within the sector. Although a particularly high yield for a REIT may sometimes reflect a disproportionately high risk, there exist opportunities in some cases to capitalize on dividend yields that are sufficiently attractive to justify the underlying risks of the investment. I have included below a table ranking equity REITs from highest dividend yield (as of 6/30/2025) to lowest dividend yield.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Although a REIT’s decision regarding whether to pay a quarterly dividend or a monthly dividend does not reflect on the quality of the company’s fundamentals or operations, a monthly dividend allows for smoother cash flow to the investor. Below is a list of equity REITs that pay monthly dividends ranked from highest yield to lowest yield.

Dividend News

7 REITs announced dividend hikes in June, 6 of which pay quarterly and 1 of which pays monthly.: The largest dividend increase came from Sun Communities (SUI) (+10.6%). In total, 45 REITs increased their dividend during the first half of 2025.

Economic Health

The number of corporate bankruptcies decreased slightly month-over-month but remained elevated in June. There were more filings year-to-date in 2025 than in the first 6 months of any other year since 2010. There have been 10.7% more bankruptcy filings over the first half of 2025 than during the same period in 2024.

Valuation

REIT Premium/Discount to NAV by Property Type

Below is a downloadable data table, which ranks REITs within each property type from the largest discount to the largest premium to NAV. The consensus NAV used for this table is the average of analyst NAV estimates for each REIT. Both the NAV and the share price will change over time, so I will continue to include this table in upcoming issues of The State of REITs with updated consensus NAV estimates for each REIT for which such an estimate is available.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Takeaway

The large cap REIT premium (relative to small cap REITs) narrowed in June and investors are now paying on average about 32% more for each dollar of 2025 FFO/share to buy large cap REITs than small cap REITs (17.6x/13.3x - 1 = 32.3%). As can be seen in the table below, there is presently a strong positive correlation between market cap and FFO multiple.

The table below shows the average NAV premium/discount of REITs of each market cap bucket. This data, much like the data for price/FFO, shows a strong, positive correlation between market cap and Price/NAV. The average large cap REIT (-4.41%) trades at a single-digit discount to consensus NAV and mid cap REITs (-10.52%) trade at a low double-digit discount. Small cap REITs (-26.74%) trade at just under 3/4 of NAV while micro caps (-40.90%) trade at only about 3/5 of their respective NAVs.

71% of FFO Per Share Guidance Midpoint Adjustments Announced During the 2nd Quarter Were Upward Revisions

Among REITS that provided 2025 FFO per share guidance, 63.5% did not announce a change to their FFO per share guidance midpoint during Q2 2025. Among the 36.5% that did, however, 71% raised guidance while only 29% revised downward.

Downward revisions came overwhelmingly out of the Hotel and Communications sectors. The largest FFO per share guidance cuts came from Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) (-6.6%), Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) (-5.4%) and RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) (-5.1%).

By far the largest FFO per share guidance midpoint hike came from Sun Communities (SUI) (+32.5%). However, this is somewhat misleading due to the fact that the initially announced core FFO per share guidance did not include FFO from the marina portfolio that SUI would exit in a massive $5.65 billion transaction. SUI stated for illustrative purposes that if the FFO from the marina portfolio had been included in the initial 2025 core FFO guidance then the range would have been between $6.82 and $7.06 with a midpoint of $6.94. Considering this, full year 2025 guidance was effectively revised downward (-5.9%) when inclusive of the transaction.

Excluding SUI, the largest guidance increases came from CareTrust REIT (CTRE) (+3.8%), Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) (+3.2%) and Alexander & Baldwin (ALEX) (+3.0%). HOST was the only Hotel REIT to raise full year 2025 FFO per share guidance during Q2 while 6 of their Hotel REIT peers revised downward.

S&P Global Market Intelligence has forecasted dividend hikes for 18 equity REITs, 16 of which are paid quarterly and 2 of which are paid monthly. They forecast that the largest dividend increases during Q3 2025 will come from RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) (+13.3%), Alexander & Baldwin (ALEX) (+11.1%) and Welltower (WELL) (+9.0%).

Well-managed REITs are often able to increase their dividend distributions to shareholders over time and are sometimes even obligated to do so due to REIT structure rules pertaining to dividend distribution of taxable income. With rising FFO/share and dividend increases across much of the REIT sector, many REITs are well positioned for a strong total return performance over the remainder of the year.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GDXY Provides Income At A Cost
data.description:
The YieldMax Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:GDXY) is a managed ETF designed to provide investors with current income through high-yielding, monthly distributions.

GDXY employs an options trading strategy on the underlying investment strategy, the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) in order to generate returns to investors. At any given time, GDXY may take a long or short position in call & put options in order to generate returns. This allows GDXY to benefit from both the upside and downside risk to the underlying GDX ETF.

GDXY is currently engaged in vertical spreads in order to generate value. A vertical spread is when the trader buys and sells options of the same class, either calls or puts, across strike prices or expiration dates. In this case, GDXY is short July 25, 2025 Calls at a $52 strike price and long July 25, 2025 Calls at a $54 strike price. This means that GDXY has short exposure to GDX at $52/share with the potential for shares to be called away if the option is exercised. In turn, GDXY has long exposure to GDX at $54/share, meaning that GDXY will have the right but not the obligation to exercise the option if the strike price is met. The long position acts as protection against the short position, minimizing GDXY’s unlimited downside risk if GDX were to substantially appreciate in value beyond the strike price of $52/share. For example, if GDX shares were to appreciate to $55/share upon the term date, GDXY can exercise its $54 Calls to cover the $52 delivery without having to purchase shares at $55/share only to turn around and sell them at $52/share.

Another benefit of this strategy is that the $52 Call options can be sold at a relatively higher premium when compared to the $54 Call options purchased, meaning that GDXY can benefit most when options aren’t exercised. This is the core feature that allows GDXY to provide investors the income component of the ETF. The difference between the premium paid to GDXY and premium protection provides a profit for the ETF.

In general, GDXY trades closely to GDX during short periods of time.

Because of the construct of the strategy and high distribution rate, GDXY shares can face significant value decay from a share price perspective during an extended period of time.

Comparing GDXY to GDX on a total return basis, GDXY can perform relatively close to GDX, but still faces some risk value decay.

GDXY also caps potential gains if GDX shares increase in value. This can provide investors with certain loss of value over time, particularly when working in tax implications from the income component of the strategy. For example, the most recent distribution contained 95.87% return of capital and 4.13% income. Return of capital can lower an investor’s cost basis, resulting in a higher tax upon the sale of GDXY shares. Given the general value decay of the ETF, investors can potentially strategically exit GDXY positions to minimize the impact; I recommend speaking to your financial advisor prior to making an investment decision in GDXY.

On a trailing twelve-month basis, GDXY has returned $6.95/share, yielding 48%. Despite the substantial historical payout, investors must be aware that distributions can vary significantly month-to-month, potentially creating certain challenges for financial planning from an income standpoint.

Given the options strategy, the portfolio’s performance may vary from month-to-month depending on performance relative to GDX. This exposes investors to management risk, leading investors to be dependent on the skills employed by the traders at YieldMax.

GDXY currently has $100mm in assets under management with an average of 200k shares trading daily, providing investors with substantial liquidity for entering and exiting positions with minimal spread risk of roughly 11bps.

GDXY charges a fee of 1.08%, on par with the managed strategies.

Risks Related to GDXY

GDXY employs a managed options strategy in order to generate high-yielding distributions for investors. There are a variety of risks to consider before making a final investment decision, including management risk, strategy risk, distribution risk, and value decay.

Because this is a managed strategy, the success of the ETF is dependent on management’s ability to execute effectively on the strategy. This goes counter to index ETFs that track an index where management risk falls on the investor. The strategy’s performance can also be impacted by heightened levels of volatility, resulting in differentiated returns for investors.

The ETF’s performance can also impact distributions, which have historically varied from period to period. GDXY also caps upside potential if GDX shares appreciate in value. This can result in investors realizing limited upside potential while being exposed to all downside risk. This can result in substantial value decay over time and limit investors’ ability to recover losses.

Final Thoughts

Given the risks of performance balanced with the income component of the fund, I am recommending GDXY with a HOLD rating. I recommend investors discuss whether GDXY is an appropriate investment vehicle with a financial advisor prior to making a final investment decision as this strategy may not be suitable for everyone. Given the risk to total returns, GDXY may not be an optimal solution and may potentially result in losses as a result of the capped upside potential.

data.title: Ecolab: Great Business But Shares Are Priced Accordingly (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in 2022, when I last covered Ecolab (NYSE:ECL) here at Seeking Alpha, I argued that it was a high-quality compounder that was temporarily mispriced and undervalued.

At the time, ECL stock had sold off sharply due to pandemic-related disruptions. Ecolab is involved in sanitation, water treatment, pest control and other such related services. Not surprisingly, Ecolab has significant exposure to leisure and hospitality-related businesses such as restaurants, hotels, shopping centers, and theme parks where large numbers of people congregate. As you can see, Ecolab has a prestigious list of top clients, but it's also somewhat skewed toward consumer spending:

So, Ecolab seemingly had an outsized negative impact from the pandemic when those types of businesses were forced to temporarily curtail their operations.

Ecolab stock came into a double discount as it saw both its earnings per share and valuation multiple decline concurrently due to investor worries around the business. In 2022, I suggested that the stock would ultimately go back to a 35 to 40 times P/E multiple because Ecolab was a high-quality compounder with a long growth runway in a highly fragmented industry. Furthermore, Ecolab has surprisingly little competition at scale with it being by far the largest player in the industry globally.

Ecolab's nature as a long-term compounder has indeed proven to still be true. The company bounced back from the pandemic dip and has now resumed climbing along its standard upward trajectory:

But will the future be as bright for Ecolab, or are investors fully pricing in the company's strengths at today's valuation?

Ecolab Now Faces Some Intermediate-Term Obstacles

I won't beat around the bush here. My view now is that my prior thesis has fully played out and that shares are no longer undervalued. Consider the forward outlook for Ecolab's earnings:

The stock is indeed back to its typical 35-40x P/E ratio range that it traded at frequently in prior years. However, the company's growth rate has not reached levels which would usually be associated with the sort of high-quality compounders that can sustain high 30s P/E ratios. In fact, we now see analysts projecting just 2% revenue growth this year and 5% growth next year.

Management's perspective is that it can keep delivering double-digit earnings growth off that pedestrian sales growth rate due to margin expansion:

However, my counterpoint would be that the company has already scored a huge rebound in margins and EPS has moved well above pre-pandemic levels. I think Ecolab will need faster revenue growth to support management's 12-15% targeted annualized EPS growth.

In addition, I believe a couple of factors of now act negatively against the company's intermediate-term growth outlook as compared to back in 2022. These factors make me less optimistic, and thus I'm moving to a hold rating.

First off, a significant portion of both Ecolab's growth potential and valuation multiple is arguably related to government policy. Ecolab is one of the largest companies in the United States that has significant business tied to water investments in fields like treatment, recycling, and quality. This fact has made Ecolab a staple in environmental, social, and governance ("ESG")-related portfolios. Ecolab's investor presentations emphasize its ESG credentials in terms of helping improve outcomes in water, food safety, climate, and health:

Back in 2022, ESG-related investing appeared to have a strong tailwind. There were many new ESG funds launching and the strategy was gaining assets under management at a rapid clip. Today, however, at least from my vantage point, it appears that ESG investing has lost some of that prior momentum. I don't see inflows from ESG asset managers having the same impact on Ecolab and other top sustainability picks going forward that it had a few years ago.

Furthermore, I don't see the Trump Administration as being especially pre-disposed to channeling investments into categories such as water quality or the treatment of forever chemicals. In fact, it appears that the current Environmental Protection Agency ("EPA") leadership may delay regulations on forever chemicals which would likely reduce the amount of money flowing into water treatment investments.

We could easily see interest in this field come back with the next administration, so it's something to watch with the next election cycle and beyond. But for now, the recent tax and spending bill didn't appear to have much in the way of favorable news for Ecolab, and I don't expect a meaningful government spending tailwind from the current administration.

Another concern is with high interest rates. Back in 2022, interest rates had surged but that seemed tied to the pandemic-related supply chain uncertainties. Back in 2022, I had been expecting that long-term interest rates would have begun to meaningfully decline by the time we reached 2025. Today, however, interest rates remain at relatively high levels, at least compared to the past decade.

The bond market has once again looked jittery in 2025 with the yields on longer term U.S. government bond and various international government bonds remaining near multi-year highs. This lowers the valuation multiple that investors are typically willing to assign to a steady, long-term compounder story such as Ecolab.

Put another way, people are a lot more willing to pay a 35x P/E multiple when interest rates are near zero as opposed to when the risk-free rate is near 5%. In addition, Ecolab has about $7 billion in long-term debt, and it historically has used leverage to finance acquisitions as part of its continuing M&A strategy. The company's debtload is perfectly manageable in comparison to the size of the business, but $7 billion is chunky enough that higher rates could create a meaningful drag from increased interest expenses over time.

ECL Stock Bottom Line

Combining all these factors, I believe Ecolab's intermediate-term outlook is not especially strong. Rather, ECL stock is likely trading at a 36x P/E multiple today in large part because the overall S&P 500 is trading at all-time highs rather than investors being especially enthusiastic about Ecolab's outlook in particular. And, if the S&P 500 corrected, I think ECL stock would sell off considerably as well.

I still think that Ecolab has a tremendous business model. It's the only player at scale internationally that can serve as a sort of one-stop shop across sanitation, hygiene, water treatment, and pest control.

Notably, Ecolab's closest peer, Diversey, was taken private once again in 2023. I had been more concerned about Diversey as a competitive risk when it was a publicly traded company that was focused on growth. However, under private market ownership, I'd guess Diversey will focus more on maximizing cash flows from existing business rather than pursuing aggressive growth which could derail Ecolab's ambitions.

Long story short, there continues to be a huge amount of M&A opportunity here. Per Ecolab management, they only touch 11% of the total addressable market but are twice as large as the nearest peer:

This means that Ecolab has a huge range of suitable M&A targets but also that it shouldn't face strict anti-trust pushback on potential deals as the market isn't that consolidated yet.

I have little doubt that Ecolab will be a much larger and more profitable business in 2035 than it is today. I'm content to hold my ECL stock position in my buy-and-hold portfolio.

That said, I don't see much upside over the next 12-24 months. Earnings growth is likely to be fairly pedestrian, especially in the near-term with 2025 revenues projected to rise less than 2%. Meanwhile, I think current macroeconomic conditions will limit how much the valuation multiple can expand from an already healthy 36x starting P/E.

As such, ECL stock merits a hold rating for the time being. The company's long-term growth algorithm remains robust. And I expect growth may pick up again in the future, especially if we see more federal spending on items such as water quality. But shares are fully valued right now and I'm not thinking about adding to my position at current price levels.

data.title: Fox Factory: Shares Have Dropped Too Far (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in June of 2023, I decided it was time to downgrade Fox Factory Holding Corp. (NASDAQ:FOXF) from a Buy to a Hold. This decision was based on the fact that the company had started exhibiting weakness in certain areas. Shares were also no longer attractive based on valuation.

However, it seems as though I didn't downgrade it enough. Since downgrading it, the stock has fallen an astounding 71.3%. That has come at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 47.3%. Looking at the data available, we can see a continuous decline in revenue, profits, and cash flows. Early this year, we started to see some recovery. And under the assumption that the markets in which it operates will do better in the future than they have in the past, I can understand why some investors would be optimistic.

Ultimately, I view Fox Factory as a speculative play at this moment. Leverage has certainly risen over the last few years now, and bottom line results are still struggling for the most part. However, there are signs of light at the end of the tunnel, and a very specific part of the company has been growing nicely. For those who don't mind taking on risk, I think that this opportunity could very well play out nicely. But it will require patience and an appetite for volatility that many investors might not have. Due to this, I am upgrading it to a soft and speculative Buy rating.

Taking stock of Fox Factory

For those not familiar with Fox Factory, the company operates as a designer, engineer, manufacturer, and marketer, of certain products and systems that are used on bikes, off-road vehicles, trucks, and more. Some of the products it focuses on include snowmobiles, ATVs, etc. To truly understand the company, however, it would be helpful to look into each of its operating segments and what they do.

The largest of these at the moment is the Powered Vehicles Group, which focuses on providing suspension components for all sorts of vehicles such as off-road vehicles, powered vehicles like ATVs, and more. The firm also has another segment called the Aftermarket Applications Group, which provides suspension systems and lift kits on an aftermarket basis. It also services the needs of customers looking to upfit their vehicles and it provides aftermarket wheels. Lastly, there is the Specialty Sports Group, which focuses on bike suspension components, as well as baseball and softball gear and equipment.

Over the last few years now, Fox Factory has had an undeniably tough time. From 2022 to 2023, revenue for the company dropped from $1.60 billion to $1.46 billion. It then declined further to $1.39 billion in 2024. This weakness was driven by pain across two of its three operating segments. However, there is a lot of nuance that is needed here. As the chart below illustrates, financial performance for each of its operating segments from a revenue standpoint has been quite volatile. Given the time has passed, and the fact that data from a few years ago is not as relevant as data today is, I will mostly focus on the 2023 to 2024 window.

During this time, revenue associated with the Powered Vehicles Group dropped from $523.9 million to $461.4 million. Management claimed that this 11.9% drop was the result of lower demand in power sports and automotive spaces caused by high interest rates that negatively affected end market demand. They also cited higher inventory levels at dealerships. This certainly would benefit from some context. Because of the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a surge in buying activity, both at the consumer level and at the company level. This caused excess inventories once everything slowed down, leading to inventory destocking, not only for this space, but for many industries that I have looked at.

Similarly, there was a decline associated with the Aftermarket Applications Group, with sales contracting from $551.1 million to $421.4 million. This 23.5% plunge was caused by the lower up fitting sales that management blamed on product mix, chassis availability, high interest rates affecting end users and dealers, and higher inventory levels at dealerships. Lastly, we had the Specialty Sports Group. During this time, revenue actually jumped 31.3% from $389.2 million to $511.1 million.

Things would have been better had it not been for lower end consumer demand and a $53.7 million reduction in sales associated with inventory recalibration for its ongoing bike channel business. The rise, then, came from the firm's purchase, in November of last year, of Marucci, a producer of baseball, softball, and other sports related products. That particular purchase cost shareholders $567.2 million and accounted for the vast majority of the $717.5 million in acquisitions that the company made throughout the 2022 through 2024 windows.

Naturally, a decline in revenue the magnitude of which we saw over the last three years would bring with it a decline in profitability also. Net income plummeted from $205.3 million to $6.6 million. On an adjusted basis, which strips out impairment charges and other one-time items, we would still get a fall from $232.7 million to $55.4 million. Operating cash flow dropped from $187.1 million to $131.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $259 million to $90.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business pulled back from $321.8 million to $167 million.

The good news for shareholders is that we have started to see some recovery in overall sales. Revenue in the first quarter of 2025 came in at $355 million. That represents a 6.5% rise over the $333.5 million generated one year earlier.

Interestingly, every single one of its three operating segments experienced sales growth during this time. For instance, the Powered Vehicles Group reported a 3.4% rise in revenue from $118.1 million to $122.1 million. This was only because, however, the firm benefited from its acquisition of Marzocchi Back in December of 2024. It ultimately added $12 million in revenue this year compared to last year. Once again, weakness in end user demand caused by higher interest rates and excess inventory levels negatively affected organic revenue.

When it comes to the Aftermarket Applications Group, the company reported a 9.9% rise in sales from $101.9 million to $111.9 million. This actually is really interesting because it was not due to acquisition activity. Instead, management mentioned that upfitting sales increased thanks to changes in product mix and higher demand for aftermarket products. The firm did acknowledge that higher interest rates and excess inventory levels at dealerships continue to be problematic. But still, sales growth is sales growth all the same. Lastly, the Specialty Sports Group reported a 6.6% jump in revenue from $113.5 million to $121 million. This was in spite of the fact that the company continued to suffer from channel inventory recalibration and lower end consumer demand headwinds. It was driven, according to the company, by bike sales growing.

Year over year, profitability was largely lower. For instance, the company went from a net loss of $3.5 million in the first quarter of 2024 to a net loss of $259.7 million the same time this year. This was primarily the result, however, of a $262.1 million goodwill impairment charge caused by industry weakness. Other profitability metrics were still lower, but nowhere near as much. For instance, adjusted net profits contracted only marginally from $11.9 million to $9.8 million. A big driver here seems to have been a jump in research and development expenditures from $14.4 million, or 4.33% of sales, to $17 million, or 4.80%. Operating cash flow plummeted from $9.4 million to just $0.7 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a dip from $25.8 million to $24.1 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business dipped from $40.4 million to $39.6 million.

Management seems to have a mixed view of 2025. They are currently expecting, for instance, for revenue to be between $1.385 billion and $1.485 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 2.9% above what the company generated last year. Continued improvements in the Aftermarket Applications Group and the Specialty Sports Group should combine with the aforementioned acquisition activities of the company to make this growth possible. On the bottom line, management expects adjusted earnings per share of between $1.60 and $2.60. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net income of $87.6 million compared to the $55.4 million the company reported for 2024.

Certain challenges remain for the company. The biggest one would be tariffs and the impact they might have on not only the firm's operating costs, but also on end customer demand. Management estimated in their first quarter earnings call that the gross impact of tariffs, on a full year basis, would be about $50 million. However, this situation is fluid and is changing pretty regularly because of the current Trump Administration. Management is working to address these concerns by making changes to their supply chain and hiking prices. But there is no telling what higher prices will do to end consumer demand, especially as tariffs affect the economy more broadly.

We do know that management, separate from all of this, is continuing to push for $25 million in cost cutting this year. So if that comes to fruition, it should help. It is worth noting that only 30% to 35% of these savings will be achieved in the first half of this year, with the second half accounting for the bulk of the savings.

Because of the volatility and the lack of more detailed guidance for 2025, I think it would be best to instead value the company based on results from the last couple of years. In the chart above, you can see how shares are currently priced using these metrics. Honestly, even if we assume that the 2024 data represents a new normal for the company, the picture isn't bad. The stock would be perhaps fairly valued in this case. And if we do assume that results this year will look better than last year, it could certainly tilt the company in the direction of being undervalued on an absolute basis. I also, in the table below, compared it to five similar firms. Using each of the two valuation metrics, I found that two of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Fox Factory Holding Corp 12.3 10.7
XPEL Inc. (XPEL) 17.3 13.1

Dorman Products (DORM)

 16.2 10.8
Standard Motor Products (SMP) 11.7 9.9
Gentherm (THRM) 9.0 6.7
Modine Manufacturing (MOD) 23.1 14.0

One thing that investors should continue to take into consideration would be the overall leverage of the company. Net debt skyrocketed from $54.8 million in 2022 to $659.9 million in 2023 because of managements acquisition activities. Net debt has risen even further to $671.3 million as of the first quarter of this year, bringing the net leverage ratio of the company up to 4.02. Management has stressed that reducing leverage is their number one priority this year. In fact, by allocating cash flows generated from operations, the firm is targeting a reduction to a net leverage ratio of 3 by the end of the 2025 fiscal year.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I can definitely understand why investors have been pessimistic over the last couple of years now. Financial performance has been disappointing and leverage has increased. The good news is that the worst seems to be over, absent a major decline in consumer demand. To be honest with you, I do think that we are heading into a recession. But with the recent data provided by management, and the firm's decision to focus on cutting debt moving forward, I think that this makes for a good speculative Buy candidate.

data.title: OptimizeRx: Stock Could Rise As Profitability Is Achieved
data.description:
OptimizeRx (NASDAQ:OPRX) is expected to turn a profit in 2025 as the company is growing revenue at a strong double-digit pace. OPRX is also reducing operating expenses which should drive margin expansion. The stock appears to be bottoming from a multiple-year sharp sell-off. I expect continued strong growth to drive the stock for strong gains over the next year.

OptimizeRx Company Background

OptimizeRx offers various solutions as a digital healthcare technology company. OPRX connects over 2 million healthcare providers [HCPs] with millions of patients. This is accomplished through a proprietary omnichannel network that contains an intelligent technology platform. The company uses AI for effective marketing by aligning patients and providers. This is accomplished through predictive targeting, omnichannel activation, and synchronized placement.

OPRX's solutions effectively lead patients to the right treatment through the company's marketing solutions. The company offers direct-to-consumer [DTC] solutions for a patient-centered strategy. OptimizeRx sees a trend where there is a movement away from generic mass media consumer advertising to more focused personalized interactions between HCPs and patients.

OPRX is the only company that aligns brand messaging to custom patient segments with its customer's HCP marketing using machine learning methods. The company's offering that provides this solution is known as the Dynamic Audience Activation Platform [DAAP].

The company also offers Micro-Neighborhood Targeting which uses a privacy-first process to create consumer audiences. OPRX's Profiler provides insights for its customers to target consumers. OPRX also has other related solutions.

Growth Drivers

OptimizeRx believes that it is the only company in the industry that is capable of effectively connecting doctors and patients at scale. The reason for this belief is due to OPRX's competitive advantages. These advantages include leveraging one of the largest point-of-care networks in the United States. This process enables pharmaceutical manufacturers to reach healthcare providers directly at the point of care.

Another advantage is OPRX's ability to combine its extensive network with its omni-channel technology for effective patient-finding capabilities. This is accomplished through the company's DAAP and Micro-Neighborhood technologies.

OptimizeRx is on a mission to solve the commercialization issues that the pharma industry currently faces. This includes reducing script abandonment, better brand visibility, enhancing interoperability, and supporting the growing shift to more costly & complex specialty medications.

The company's success is evident as OPRX achieved a 20% increase in contracted revenue for Q1 2025 over Q1 2024. This achievement demonstrates OptimizeRx's ability to develop strong customer relationships with them as valued partners.

OPRX's focus on operational improvements led to a $1.8 million year-over-year decrease in operational expenses. This was driven by the cost-savings that the company implemented over the past year. It was also driven by stock-based compensation. Further decreases in operational expenses can get OPRX to profitability.

Another key factor for future growth is OPRX's shift to a subscription model. Currently, over 5% of total revenue has been converted to subscription contracts in 2025. That leaves a lot of revenue to convert this year and in future years. As this revenue is converted to subscriptions, it can provide a steadier and more predictable stream of revenue.

OPRX's positive momentum in Q1 2025 led the company to provide positive guidance for 2025. OptimizeRx expects revenue for 2025 to be $101 million to $106 million and adjusted EBITDA to be $13 million to $15 million. This guidance was increased from the end of Q4 2024 when OPRX projected revenue of $100 million and adjusted EBITDA of $12 million.

The company's revised expected revenue would be 9.7% to 15% higher than 2024's revenue of $92.1 million. Adjusted EBITDA would be 11% to 28% higher than the $11.7 million from 2024. These double-digit expected gains for the top and bottom lines should help drive the stock for strong gains if they are achieved or exceeded over the next several quarters.

Valuation

OptimizeRx is trading at 25x expected EPS of $0.55 for 2025. This is lower than the forward PE of 36 for the Health Information Services industry. OPRX's lower than average forward PE puts the stock in a good position to perform well as the company continues its strong growth.

The consensus EPS expectation of $0.55 has been upgraded from $0.34 from 3 months ago. That's a 62% upgrade. This is likely one of the reasons for the stock's recent strong performance over the past few months.

The $0.55 EPS estimate can be achieved with revenue growth of about 12% with lowered operational expenses widening the operating and net margins.

Technical Perspective

OPRX's monthly chart above illustrates how the stock recently bounced higher from a long-term multiple-year support level. The RSI indicator moved into positive territory above the 50 level. The MACD is also positive as the blue line rose above the red signal line while the histogram turned green with rising bars. The RSI and MACD are both showing that the stock has positive upward momentum.

Continued strong growth can catalyze the stock higher. OPRX's next earnings report on August 7, 2025 can provide more insights into the company's growth trends. The Q2 earnings report is likely to be the next catalyst for the stock just as the positive Q1 report helped drive the stock higher over the past few months.

Risks for the Stock

OptimizeRx has been unprofitable in the past. While the company is expected to turn a profit in 2025, it is possible that unexpected conditions could challenge OPRX to turn a profit. This would likely lead to a sharp sell-off for the stock.

Since OPRX is on the verge of profitability, the company may have a need to do a capital raise to expand the business. Capital raises tend to dilute shareholders with more shares of stock. This usually has a negative impact on the stock price.

The balance sheet shows a high amount of total debt of $32.8 million as compared to total cash of $16.6 million. This leaves the company with net debt of $16.2 million. If OPRX struggles to maintain profitability the company could struggle to pay off its debt.

OptimizeRx's Long-Term Outlook

OptimizeRx's increased revenue and EBITDA guidance demonstrates confidence that the company is experiencing strong growth. The increased guidance for 2025 shows that customers are demanding OPRX's offerings.

I like OPRX's shift to a subscription model. This should smooth out revenue over time and provide the company with recurring revenue. The subscription model helps foster customer loyalty and can help OPRX to scale up.

The company's reductions in operational expenses are likely to drive operational and net income margin growth. This will help OPRX to achieve profitability and strong earnings growth.

Analysts have a one-year price target of $15.67 for the stock. This is just 12% higher than the current price. I think that this estimate is too conservative, given the strong momentum that the company and the stock are experiencing.

I think that $20 is a better target price for the stock, if OPRX does hit or exceed its revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance for the year. The target of $20 would increase the PE to 36 based on expected EPS of $0.55 for 2025, which is the industry's average forward PE. The Health Information Services industry is expected to average about 17% annual earnings growth over the next five years. OPRX is expected to average 44% annual growth for 2025 and 2026. This strong growth should drive the stock to possibly reach the industry's average valuation.
